skill.name: newsletter-curation
skill.description: Helps plan and write curated email newsletters with source discovery, issue structure, commentary guidance, sending cadence, growth tactics, and performance metrics.
skill.summary: Plan curated newsletters with sourcing workflows, editorial formats, commentary prompts, cadence advice, and growth tactics.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified a dangerous remote install pattern and networked command examples, but most shell/backtick and weak-cryptography alerts are Markdown false positives. The skill appears focused on newsletter workflow guidance, with no evidence of prompt injection or malicious data exfiltration, but it should publish with a warning about installing and running the external inference.sh CLI.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download an install script from an external URL and pipe it directly into sh. This is a real supply-chain risk because the script executes before the user can inspect it, even though the surrounding text claims checksum verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present on the flagged line. It is dangerous by behavior, although the context suggests a vendor install flow rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External CLI and Networked App Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill authorizes Bash use for infsh and provides examples that run external inference.sh apps for search, image generation, posting, and app discovery. This creates network and third-party processing exposure for newsletter topics, draft text, and social content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and example commands directly show external CLI execution. The risk is contextual data exposure, not evidence of stealthy exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Syntax Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Most static external-command findings point to Markdown fenced examples or inline Markdown placeholders. These are documentation snippets, not Ruby backtick execution, so they are false positives except where the content actually instructs command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are visible Markdown fences and prose examples. No Ruby code or executable backtick interpolation appears in those sections.

low_findings.title: Checksum References Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings do not show evidence of MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptographic implementation. The visible checksum reference is SHA-256 verification text, which is not weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines found newsletter prose, install verification text, and package install examples rather than weak cryptographic code. Confidence is slightly reduced because the static pattern names did not include the exact matched token.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The skill includes external inference.sh and dist.inference.sh links. These are expected for a CLI-based workflow, but they require trust in external domains and should be disclosed to users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly visible in the documented install flow. They are not suspicious by themselves, but they support the network risk assessment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Install Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The command downloads a remote script and executes it through sh in one step. Users should prefer manual download, inspection, and checksum verification before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous command structure is explicitly present. This is a well-known supply-chain risk even when the source domain is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples send queries and content to external infsh apps such as search, image rendering, social posting, and app listing. Sensitive topics or unpublished newsletter drafts could leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly call external app runners with user-provided text. The pattern is legitimate for this skill, but it has clear privacy and network implications.

content.value_statement: Newsletter creators often lose time finding sources, shaping commentary, and choosing a repeatable issue format. This skill provides practical structures, prompts, cadence guidance, and growth tactics for curated email newsletters.
actual_capabilities: Provides newsletter formats for link roundups, deep dives, essays, interviews, and data-focused issues.
Offers a reusable issue structure with intro, featured story, curated links, quick hits, metrics, and closing sections.
Suggests content sourcing workflows using inference.sh search apps and topical search prompts.
Gives commentary formulas for adding context, reader value, and editorial perspective to curated links.
Summarizes cadence, subject line, growth, and performance metric guidance for newsletter operations.
limitations: Requires user judgment to verify source credibility and factual accuracy.
Uses external inference.sh CLI examples that may send topics or draft text to third-party services.
Does not provide email service provider setup, list management, or deliverability automation.
Marketing benchmarks are general guidance and may not match every audience or niche.

use_cases.title: Plan a Weekly Industry Digest
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable weekly issue with a lead story, curated links, quick hits, and a clear reader takeaway.

use_cases.title: Add Editorial Value to Links
use_cases.description: Turn sourced articles into useful commentary by explaining context, relevance, and a concise editorial point of view.

use_cases.title: Improve Newsletter Growth Workflow
use_cases.description: Use subject line, cadence, archive, referral, and social teaser guidance to improve acquisition and engagement.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Newsletter Format
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a newsletter format for a weekly issue about [topic]. Compare link roundup, deep dive, original essay, and data/trends formats. Recommend one format and outline the sections.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Curated Link Roundup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a link roundup for [audience] using these sources: [paste links and notes]. For each item, write a short headline, one useful summary sentence, and one editorial takeaway.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Full Issue Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a newsletter issue for [newsletter name] about [theme]. Include a short intro, one big story, five worth-reading links, three quick hits, a stat of the week, and a concise closing.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Newsletter Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this newsletter plan: [paste plan]. Evaluate content quality, source mix, commentary depth, subject line strength, sending cadence, growth loops, and metrics. Give prioritized improvements.

output_examples.input: A creator asks for a weekly AI newsletter structure.
output_examples.output: Recommended format: deep dive plus links. Sections include a short personal intro, one main analysis, five curated reads with commentary, three quick hits, one metric, and a reader question.

output_examples.input: An editor provides five article links about developer tools.
output_examples.output: The skill turns each link into a concise title, a one-sentence summary, and a practical note explaining why the article matters to engineering leaders.

output_examples.input: A marketer wants to grow a newsletter archive.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends consistent weekly publishing, searchable web archives, social teasers, referral prompts, and tracking open rate, click rate, replies, forwards, and unsubscribe rate.

best_practices: Verify every source before inclusion and add original commentary to each curated item.
Use a consistent weekly structure so readers know what to expect.
Inspect external install scripts and avoid sending confidential drafts to networked tools.
anti_patterns: Publishing long lists of links without context or a clear editorial point of view.
Changing cadence often enough that readers cannot build a habit.
Using remote CLI commands on private topics or unpublished content without review.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create curated email newsletters, weekly digests, link roundups, deep dives, and social teasers.

faq.question: Does it find sources automatically?
faq.answer: It includes inference.sh search command examples, but users should verify results and source quality themselves.

faq.question: Can it write commentary for each link?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides a formula for explaining what happened, why it matters, and the editor perspective.

faq.question: Does it send email campaigns?
faq.answer: No. It does not manage subscribers, deliverability, email templates, or sending inside an email platform.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: The skill documents external CLI commands, including a remote install script. Users should inspect scripts and avoid sharing sensitive drafts.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
